questions.description: Outline the roles of the various European Union institutions in EU law-making and assess how these roles were changed…
questions.text: Outline the roles of the various European Union institutions in EU law-making and assess how these roles were changed by the Lisbon Treaty amendments. Pay particular attention to the changes that have been made to the roles of: (i) The European Parliament; (ii) The Commission; (iii) The Court of Justice of the European Union; and (iv) The Council. Discuss whether or not, and in what ways, these Lisbon Treaty changes have made the European Union a more democratic organisation, referencing, where appropriate, relevant post 1 December 2009 case-law.

questions.description: Article 3 of Directive 2010/123/EU (fictional) states that: "Member States… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: Article 3 of Directive 2010/123/EU (fictional) states that: "Member States shall ensure that the substances set out in Annex I are not included in any paint-stripper after the date laid down in Article 15 of this Directive." Annex I includes a reference to the substance 'dichloromethane'. Article 15 sets the date for implementation as 30 November 2011. Dichloromethane was banned from use in paint-stripper by Directive 2010/123/EU due to the serious risks that it presents to human health, including death in some circumstances. A range of scientific studies, including some undertaken by the EU Commission's own scientific committees and advisory bodies, have shown that it can cause cancer when inhaled on a regular or ongoing basis. These reports have demonstrated that the inclusion of dichloromethane at any level above 50 parts per million is potentially dangerous for human health and it was therefore recommended that an outright prohibition on the use of this substance in paint-stripper and similar products should be introduced. The Minister for Health in Ireland introduced the Paint and Paint Products (Toxic Substances) Regulations 2012 (fictional), which came into effect on 31 March 2012. Regulation 4 of these 2012 Irish Regulations provides that: "Dichloromethan e shall not be permitted for inclusion in paint-stripper where the quantity present exceeds 100 parts per million."· Frank, who is employed by Brighter Homes Ltd. as a painter and decorator, has just been diagnosed with lung cancer, which he believes may have been caused by over- exposure to dichloromethane. His medical consultant also thinks that the inhalation of dichloromethane over the past decade or so is likely to have played a significant role in Frank's development of this disease. When Frank raises the issue with0Brighter Homes Ltd. they insist that all of the paint-stripping products that they have used since 2012 were in compliance with the Irish Regulations. They are able to clearly substantiate this claim. Frank's solicitor insists that Directive 2010/123/EU has direct effect and that either Frank's employers or the State must compensate him for this unlawful exposure to the substance dichloromethane . Advise Frank on whether he may be able to bring a claim against either (i) Brighter Homes Ltd. or (ii) the Department of the Minister for Health for a failure to comply with the provisions of Directive 2010/123/EU on dichloromethane use in paint- strippers. In so doing, discuss the nature of the claims that may be brought and refer to relevant CJEU case-law in particular.

questions.description: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on…
questions.text: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C- 50/2000, UPA v Council, that: "[. ..] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC by a Community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, a· substantial adverse effect on his interests" He was clearly articulating that there was a lack of effective judicial protection for non privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union's institutions. Explain briefly the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing here. Also, make an assessment of whether these concerns have been fully addressed by the changes that were made to Article 230 EC by the Lisbon Treaty and now contained in Article 263 TFEU and/or any developments that may have come from the related case law of the Union judicature on this matter, and in particular the case-law interpreting the new post-Lisbon fourth paragraph to Article 263 TFEU.

questions.description: have been making and selling bread in Ireland since 2014. Regulation.
questions.text: Bob the Baker Ltd. have been making and selling bread in Ireland since 2014. Their range of products includes the 'Traditional Sliced Pan', the 'Farmhouse Irish Soda Bread' and the 'Artisan Rye Bread'. Bob the Baker Ltd. also export around 25,000 loaves of their 'Farmhouse Irish Soda Bread' to Germany every week. All of their other sales are on the domestic Irish market. Bob the Baker Ltd. have just been informed by the Irish Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine that terms such as 'Traditional', 'Farmhouse' and 'Artisan' can no longer be used by any bread manufacturer who produces more than 15,000 loaves of bread per week and that they must therefore cease to use these terms for their products. These descriptive terms are reserved under Regulation 7 of the (fictional) Irish Bread Regulations 2015 for use by small-scale producers only. EU Regulation 1169/2011 on the provision of food information to consumers does not limit the use of these specific descriptive terms in any way. It does, however provide that descriptions used on food labelling should not be 'misleading' in any way. This term 'misleading as used in Regulation 1169/2011, is clarified as relating to: "the characteristics of the food and, in particular, as to its nature, identity, properties, composition, quantity, durability, country of origin or place of provenance, method of manufacture or production". Bob the Baker Ltd. want to challenge the validity of Regulation 7 of the Irish Bread Regulations 2015. The basis of its argument is that the prohibition imposed on the use of these descriptive terms for larger producers like it goes beyond what is set out in EU Regulation 1169/2011. It also contends that it is incompatible with Articles 34 and 35 TFEU on measures equivalent to quantitative restrictions on trade and that this is unjustifiable under Article 36 TFEU. You have been asked by Bob the Baker Ltd. for your opinion on what specific arguments could be made both in support of and against their claim that the Irish Bread Regulations 2015 are incompatible with EU law, and with Articles 34-36 TFEU in particular. Advise Bob the Baker Ltd., supporting your legal advice with reference to and discussion of relevant case law of the CJEU and of relevant EU legislation.

questions.description: Tara is a 37 year old actress from Dublin. Company, Director.
questions.text: Tara is a 37 year old actress from Dublin. In early December 2018 she auditioned for the lead role in a three-part television drama miniseries called 'Planespotting' that is set in Edinburgh, Scotland. The audition went very well as Tara is an excellent actress with a strong curriculum vitae who is very highly regarded in the sector and who can clearly imitate a very convincing Edinburgh accent. She was told by the casting director , Alastair, that she was " perfect for the role" and that they "would be in contact soon to make her a firm offer'. Tara did not hear anything from the film company, Ben Nevis Productions Ltd., by mid January 2019, so she decided to phone the casting director Alastair to see whether they had made a final decision on the role and when she could expect to hear definitively from the production company. At this stage Alastair told Tara that he was very sorry but that the Board of the Scottish Film and Performing Arts Council (SFPAC) (fictional), who are funding the production of the miniseries have insisted that all of the performers and crew must come from within a 30-mile radius of Edinburgh. The SFPAC are entirely State-funded. They are directed by a Board of seven members, four of whom are appointed by the Scottish Minister for Sport and Culture (fictional). Tara is looking for some advice on whether this decision to employ local actors only is compatible with European Union rules on the free movement of workers, as set out in Article 45 TFEU, any relevant EU secondary legislation and the relevant case law of the CJEU. Advise Tara on whether she can bring a claim that the refusal of Ben Nevis Productions Ltd. to employ her in their production of 'Planespotting' is incompatible with Article 45 TF.EU and related case law of the CJEU and EU secondary legislation.

questions.description: Write a critical assessment of the way in which the status of human rights in the European Union legal order has…
questions.text: Write a critical assessment of the way in which the status of human rights in the European Union legal order has changed since the foundation of the EEC in the 1950s. Refer to relevant case law of the CJEU and to EU primary law (particularly the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU) and any other EU legislative initiatives that have played a role in bringing about this level of change.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any [THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any [THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgement: (i) Case C-79/93 Bayerische Motorenwerke AG v ALO Auto/easing D GmbH (1995] ECR 1- 3439; (ii) Cases C-468-478/06Sot Lelos kai Sia EE and Others v GlaxoSmithKline AEVE Farmaketikon Proi'onton [2008] ECR 1- 71 39; (iii) Case 112/84 Humblot v Directeur des Services Fiscaux [1985] ECR 1367; (iv) Case 87/75 Bresciani v Amministrazione de/le Finanze [1976] ECR 129; (v) Case C-438/05 International Transport Workers Federation (ITF) and Finnish Seamen's Union (FSU) v Viking Line ABP and 00 Viking Line Eesti [2007] ECR 1- 779; (vi) Case C-372/04,Watts v Bedford Primary Care Trust [2006] ECR 1-4325; (vii) Case C-236/09 Association Beige des Consommateurs Test-Achats v Conseil des Ministries (2011] ECR 1- 773; (viii) Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Siochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLl:EU:C:2018:90

questions.description: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper, entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear…
questions.text: The 2014 EU Commission White Paper, entitled 'Towards more effective EU merger control' (COM (2014) 449) makes it clear that, in its opinion: "[. ..] merger control at the· EU level was strengthened by the 2004 Merger Regulation reforms, particularly the introduction of the SIEG test". But also that: "[m]ore immediately, however, there are two main ways to improve the Merger Regulation through more limited amendments. First, the Commission considers bringing acquisitions of non-controlling minority shareholdings into the scope of EU merger control. Second, there is room to further streamline case referrals in the light of the Commission's experience with the 2004 reform". Consider, by reference where appropriate, to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, how this review of merger policy might affect the way in which EU law in this area has developed since the introduction of the original Mergers Regulation in 1989 (Regulation 4064/89) and subsequent amendments, including Regulation 139/2004?

questions.description: Outline and discuss, referring where apposite to relevant Union judicature case law, the most significant changes that…
questions.text: Outline and discuss, referring where apposite to relevant Union judicature case law, the most significant changes that were made by the Lisbon Treaty amendments to the internal organisation of the EU institutions and to their respective powers .

questions.description: The Court of Justice in Case C-603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy…
questions.text: The Court of Justice in Case C-603/2010, Pelati v Slovenia, clearly summarised the level of procedural autonomy available for Member States in deciding what remedies should be available at national level for breaches of European Union law when it stated in paragraph 23 of the Judgment that: "[. ..] in accordance with settled case-law of the Court, in the absence of relevant European Union rules, the detailed procedural rules designed to ensure the protection of the rights which individuals acquire under European Union law are a matter for the domestic legal order of each Member State, in accordance with the principle of the procedural autonomy of the Member States, provided that they are not less favourable than those governing similar domestic situations and that they do not render impossible in practice or excessively difficult the exercise of rights conferred by the European Union legal order." How have these principles of 'equivalence' and 'practical possibility' referred to above been developed by the Union judicature to provide clear guidance for national courts on how they should deal with breaches of EU law?

questions.description: Article 11(f) of Regulation (EU) 2019/6 of the European Parliament and of… Regulation.
questions.text: Article 11(f) of Regulation (EU) 2019/6 of the European Parliament and of the Council on veterinary medicinal products [2019) OJ L 4/43, provides that the labelling of the outer packaging of veterinary medicinal products should: 'in the case of homeopathic veterinary medicinal products include the statement "homeopathic veterinary medicinal product". ' 0Based in County Meath, ZooZoo Ltd. are the largest producer of homeopathic veterinary medicinal products in the European Union, accounting for nearly three- quarters of all homeopathic veterinary products sold across the entire EU in 2018. However, a series of articles have been published in national newspapers in several EU Member States over the past few months (fictional), suggesting that the use of homeopathic remedies for the treatment of ill animals is cruel due to a lack of evidence supporting claims that they help in the treatment of many ailments and that they are incapable of dulling pain that animals may be suffering. ZooZoo Ltd. are concerned that an obligation to clearly indicate on the label of their products that they are homeopathic may cause severe detriment to their business. ZooZoo Ltd. point out that there is much evidence to support their own claims about the ability of homeopathic veterinary medicines to treat illness in animals and they argue that their products are being unfairly targeted for this additional labelling obligation set out in Article 11(f) of Regulation 2019/6. Referring to relevant case law and legislation, advise ZooZoo Ltd. about whether they may be able to seek a review of the legality of this provision of Regulation 2019/6 before the Union judicature.

questions.description: Directive 2016/123/EU of the European parliament and of the Council on the… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: Directive 2016/123/EU of the European parliament and of the Council on the prudential supervision of credit institutions (fictional) establishes a number of new requirements for Member States, designed to ensure that only approved moneylenders are allowed to provide financial credit to customers. Article 9(1) of Directive 2016/123 specifically provides that: "[. ..] the competent authorities shall refuse authorisation to commence the activity of a credit institution where the initial capital is less that EUR 6 million". As an exception to this, Article 9(4) of the Directive further provides that: "[m]ember States may grant authorisation to credit institutions where the initial capital is less than that specified in paragraph 1, subject to the following conditions: the initial capital is no less than EUR 1 million; and the Member State notifies the Commission of their reasons for exercising this option". Article 13 of the Directive provides that the directors of credit institutions should not be paid any bonus in a year where the institution has failed to make a profit in excess of EUR 1 million. Article 60 of Directive 2016/123/EU states that it was to come into effect by 1 August 2016. The Bank of Baltinglass was granted authorisation to operate credit, and other, facilities by the Central Bank of Ireland on 22 September 2016, despite having an initial capital investment of only EUR 3 million. The EU Commission was never notified about this authorisation. The Supervision of Credit Institutions (Amendment) Regulations 2016, transposing Directive 2016/123/EU, were not introduced until 3 December 2016. In January 2019, following a series of poor lending decisions and the award of large bonuses to the directors of the Bank of Baltinglass, which never made any profits, it has now gone into liquidation. Secure Safes Ltd. are owed €155,750 by the Bank of Baltinglass for the supply and installation of timelock safes during 2018. An initial assessment by the liquidator has shown that there is no chance that unsecured creditors like Secure Safes Ltd. will be paid what they are owed. Secure Safes Ltd. have had to make two staff redundant as a result of this non-payment and the future of this limited company is also now in jeopardy. Advise Secure Safes Ltd., by reference to relevant CJEU case law, about whether it can relyon the provisions of Directive 2016/123/EU in separate actions that it is bringing before the Irish courts against (i) the State; (ii) the Central Bank of Ireland; and (iii) the directors of the Bank of Baltinglass.

questions.description: The Irish Government's Minister for Transport, Tourism and Sport published… Company.
questions.text: The Irish Government's Minister for Transport, Tourism and Sport published the Road Safety Car Restraint Systems Bill (fictional) in June 2019. Section 7 of the Bill provides that all children under 150 ems in height or 36 kgs in weight must be seated in a 'double-lock; double-harness' child restraint when travelling in a car. Section 9 of the Bill provides that all child car restraints (child car seats) used in cars in Ireland must be fitted by a licensed installer. Section 10 of the Bill provides that licenses to install child car restraints can only be granted to installers who, amongst other things, have at least 8 full-time employees. MotoProtect Ltd., operating in Rush, County Dublin, have been manufacturing, selling and installing child car restraints into cars since 2004. However, they currently only employ 6 people. MotoProtect Ltd. do not currently manufacture any 'double- lock; double-harness' child restraints. They claim that to alter the manufacturing premises so that they would be capable of producing the 'double-lock; double- harness' restraints would cost them in excess of €780,000 which, given their small size, they simply can not afford to do. AutoEnfant are a car-seat manufacturing company based in Rennes, France. They currently export 45% of their child restraining car seats to Ireland. They do not currently produce any 'double-lock; double-harness' products. The Irish Minister for Transport, Tourism and Sport insists that the 'double-lock; double-harness' car seats are much safer for use in cars, provided they are installed by professional and competent installer. Both MotoProtect Ltd. and AutoEnfant have come to you for advise on whether there are potentially any issues concerning the compatibility of the recently published Road Safety Car Restraint Systems Bill 2019 with EU law - in particular that on the free movement of goods and the freedom to provide services. Advise MotoProtect Ltd. and AutoEnfant, referring to relevant CJEU case law and EU Treaty provisions and legislation.

questions.description: Referring to relevant case law of the CJEU and relevant EU Treaty provisions and legislation, critically analyse…
questions.text: Referring to relevant case law of the CJEU and relevant EU Treaty provisions and legislation, critically analyse whether the establishment of the legal concept of EU citizenship has provided a stronger basis for the rights of individuals to move to, work in, and reside in, EU Member States other than their own.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: (i) Case C-280/00, Altmark Trans GmbH and Regierungsprasidium Magdeburg v Nahverkehrsgesellschaft Altmark GmbH [2003) ECR 1-7747; (ii) Case C-370/2012, Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and the Attorney General [2012] ECLI 756; (iii) C-617/10, Aklagaren v Akerberg Fransson EU:C:2013:105; (iv) Case C-236/09, Association Beige des Consommateurs Test-Achats v Conseil des Ministries [2011) ECR 1-773; (v) 378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Slochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLI: EU:C:2018:979; (vi) Case C- ltaliana dCase 87/75, Bresiani Amministrazione Italiana delle Finanze [1976] ECR 129; (vii) Case 140/79, Chemial Farmaceutici v OAF SpA [1981] ECR 1; (viii) Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs- og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR 1 1459. (ix) Case 87/75, Bresciani v Amministrazione

Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do not attempt both parts. PART A Explain, by reference to relevant case-law, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101(1) TFEU. Also explain, by reference to relevant case-law, how a "dominant position" can be held by "one or more undertakings" under Article 102 TFEU. OR PART B By reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature concerning Article 102 TFEU, analyse the extent to which a dominant undertaking may take steps to protect its own commercial position in the market on which it is dominant, or on a related market, if its position is threatened by a rival and/or new entrant. Could such steps or protective behaviour be acceptable if the effect is specifically to strengthen or extend the undertaking's dominant market position
